perturbation.original_text: The Agency's name shall appear on all policies, premium notices, and cancellation notices to policyholders. Copies of all such items sent to policyholders shall be provided by MICOA to the Agency.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agency's name shall appear on all policies, premium notices, and cancellation notices to policyholders.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement that copies of notices be provided to the Agency reduces transparency and potentially impacts the Agency's ability to adequately serve and monitor the handling of policies, premiums, and cancellations, which is a breach of duty. This introduces a legal risk related to fiduciary responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 686A.310 addresses unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the business of insurance. By not providing the agency copies of crucial policy-related notices, MICOA may be hindering the agency's ability to properly service its clients and ensure compliance, which could be construed as an unfair practice.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandated that MICOA provide the Agency with copies of all policy-related notices sent to policyholders, which ensures transparency and allows the Agency to monitor policy handling. The changed text removes this requirement. The Law Explanation states that this could hinder the agency's ability to properly service its clients and ensure compliance, which could be construed as an unfair practice under NRS 686A.310, which covers unfair practices in settling claims. The omission of this requirement directly contradicts the aim of NRS 686A.310 to prevent unfair practices.

perturbation.original_text: In the event this Agreement is terminated for any reason, MICOA agrees to purchase from Agency, and Agency agrees to sell to MICOA Agency's ownership interest in the expirations for the MICOA insurance issued pursuant to this Agreement. The purchase price shall be two times Agency's commissions on business produced directly by Agency during the last 12 full months preceding the termination date. The purchase shall be completed within 60 calendar days after the termination date. In return for this payment, for a two-year period following the termination date, Agency will not directly or indirectly sell any professional liability insurance to any individuals or entities who were MICOA insureds in Nevada at the time of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event this Agreement is terminated for any reason, MICOA may, at its sole discretion, purchase from Agency Agency's ownership interest in the expirations for the MICOA insurance issued pursuant to this Agreement. The purchase price will be determined by MICOA.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to make the purchase of expirations discretionary and removing the fixed price creates a legal contradiction. Nevada law protects agencies' ownership of expirations and requires fair compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 683A.240 addresses the ownership of expirations. The original text provides a clear method for compensating the agent. The modification gives MICOA sole discretion over whether to purchase the expirations and how to value them, potentially violating the spirit of the law by depriving the agent of fair compensation for their book of business.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates MICOA to purchase Agency's ownership interest in expirations, and fixes the purchase price. The changed text makes the purchase discretionary ('MICOA may, at its sole discretion, purchase') and removes the fixed pricing ('The purchase price will be determined by MICOA.'). The Perturbation Explanation states this contradicts Nevada law protecting agencies' ownership of expirations and requiring fair compensation. The Law Explanation confirms the change gives MICOA sole discretion over the purchase and its valuation, potentially violating the spirit of the law. While the scraped snippets don't directly address 'fair compensation' or 'ownership of expirations,' the change from mandatory purchase with a fixed price to discretionary purchase with MICOA-determined pricing significantly weakens the agent's position. This is an Omission Legal Contradiction because legally mandated consumer protections are removed, so the change likely fails legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Agency must at times assure that any of its computers, data processing systems, software components, and network arrangements use for MICOA business completely and accurately, present, produce, store and calculate all dates after December 31, 1999; and that they will not produce abnormally ending or incorrect results involving such dates as used in any forward or regression data based functions. All such items must yield date-related functionalities and date fields which accurately indicate the century and millennium and correctly perform all calculations involving a four digit year field.
perturbation.changed_text: Agency will use commercially reasonable efforts to ensure its systems function adequately.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause addresses Year 2000 (Y2K) compliance. While the specific Y2K issue is outdated, the principle of ensuring reliable and accurate data processing remains relevant. Changing it to 'commercially reasonable efforts' weakens the requirement. Removing the specific stipulations contradicts Nevada's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Nevada)
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By weakening the requirement for reliable data processing systems to a vague 'commercially reasonable efforts,' MICOA is arguably undermining the agency's ability to fulfill its obligations under the agreement, thereby breaching the implied covenant. While there is no single statute, Nevada courts have consistently recognized and applied this covenant. The change introduces ambiguity and allows for potentially substandard performance that harms MICOA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original requirement for reliable data processing systems by replacing it with a vague 'commercially reasonable efforts'. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties'. The modification allows for potentially substandard performance, undermining the agency's ability to fulfill its obligations, which can be seen as a failure to act in good faith.
